subject: Statistics
title: The St. Petersburg Paradox
description: A short lesson to help you apply The St. Petersburg Paradox.

hook.text: A coin is flipped until heads. Tails on flip 1 pays $2, flip 2 pays $4, and so on. Expected value: infinity.

buildup.text: The sum of payoff × probability diverges: $1 + $1 + $1 + ... = infinity. Rationally, you should pay any price to play.

discovery.text: Nobody would pay more than about $25. Infinite expected value meets finite human willingness to pay.

twist.text: Daniel Bernoulli resolved it in 1738: people value money logarithmically. Doubling wealth doesn't double happiness.

climax.text: The paradox launched utility theory and the idea that expected value alone doesn't drive decisions.

punchline.text: Infinite expected value, finite price. Utility, not money, drives decisions.

quiz.question: How did Daniel Bernoulli resolve the St. Petersburg paradox?
options: He proposed that people value money logarithmically — doubling wealth doesn't double satisfaction
He proved the expected value was actually finite
He showed the game could never actually be played
